titlestmt.titleproper: Collection of Mellon Family and Yale Center for British Art-related Publications
Collection of Mellon Family and Yale Center for British Art-related Publications
titlestmt.author: Cate Peebles

publicationstmt.publisher: Yale Center for British Art, Institutional Archives

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Yale Center for British Art, Institutional Archives

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday July 16, 2019 at 21:00

did.unittitle: Collection of Mellon Family and Yale Center for British Art-related Publications

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Institutional Archives
Yale Center for British Art, Institutional Archives

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection contains publications related to the Yale Center for British Art and its directors, Paul Mellon, Rachel Lambert Mellon, the Mellon family, and Louis I. Kahn that have been published by entities other than the Yale Center for British Art or the Paul Mellon Centre for Studies in British Art.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Paul Mellon (1907-1999) was the son of Pittsburgh financier and former Secretary of the Treasury, Andrew W. Mellon. As a young man, Mellon developed a love for British art, literature and sport, interests that permeated his adult life and philanthropic pursuits. Mellon was a graduate of Yale College ('29) and Cambridge University, where he deepened his knowledge of British Literature and interest in equestrian sports. In partnership with his second wife, Rachel Lambert Mellon (1910 – 2013), Paul Mellon amassed a large collection of rare books and art, much of which was originally displayed at their homes in Virginia, Antigua, Washington D.C., and New York. Rachel Mellon, known as "Bunny", cultivated a life-long passion for gardening and designed the White House rose garden during the Kennedy administration. Paul Mellon's focused collecting of British art began in the late 1950s, and in 1966 he bequeathed his extensive collection of art and rare books to Yale University, along with an endowment that established the Yale Center for British Art, which opened to the public in 1977.

The Yale Center for British Art was designed by the American architect Louis I. Kahn (1901-1974) and completed after his death. The Center was Kahn's final work and is located across the street from his first commission, the Yale University Art Gallery (opened in 1953). The Center has a sleek, modern aesthetic, and it is the first museum in the United States with incorporated retail space. Notable elements of the design include the juxtaposition of materials similar in color and texture, such as the glass and steel on the façade, the strong geometry of the interior, and the many windows that bathe objects in diffused natural light. In 2005, the Center was awarded the Twenty-Five Year Award by the American Institute of Architects.

The Paul Mellon Centre for Studies in British Art, founded in 1970, is an educational charity committed to supporting original research into the history of British art and architecture of all periods. The Centre contains an extensive library and manuscript collection, with over 12,000 books about British art. It is the sister institution to the Yale Center for British Art, with which it collaborates closely, and is part of Yale University.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is available without restrictions.

corpname.part: Paul Mellon Centre for Studies in British Art
